Q1: I tried really hard to create a consistent routine, honestly. I'd set aside blocks of time in the morning and evening since I learn best by listening to lectures and discussions. With 23 hours a week dedicated to studying across 19 courses, I had to be strategic. I'd usually start by listening to recorded lectures while taking notes, then review discussion boards to hear different perspectives. It wasn't perfect, but I gave it my best shot given everything I was juggling.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I tried to keep regular morning study sessions, but when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, other weeks I'd be cramming at odd hours. With my high attendance rate, I prioritized being present for live sessions since that's where I learn best, and then I'd fit in independent study around that.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in constantly—like 44 times this term. Sometimes it was to check announcements or see if professors posted new audio materials. Other times it was pure anxiety making me check if I'd missed something important. I also logged in to connect with peers since the peer support aspect really helped me stay grounded. The LMS became like this lifeline for me to feel connected to the course.
Q4: Yes, definitely! After the first few weeks, checking the LMS became as automatic as checking my phone. I'd wake up, make coffee, and log in to see what was new. The discussion boards and peer interactions made it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with a community. Having that peer support intervention really helped cement the habit because I actually wanted to see what others were saying.
Q5: The recorded lectures and audio materials were absolutely essential for me as an auditory learner. I'd listen to them multiple times, sometimes while doing other things. The discussion posts were also really valuable because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. I watched over 214 minutes of video content, but honestly, the audio portions of those were what stuck with me most. Peer interactions also helped me process information by talking things through.
Q6: I'll be honest—I sometimes ignored the dense reading materials and text-heavy resources. As an auditory learner, walls of text just don't stick for me the same way. I also delayed some of the optional practice quizzes because I was overwhelmed with the required work. Looking back, that probably hurt my exam performance. I prioritized what I could listen to over what I had to read.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear myself understanding it better afterward. If I could explain a concept out loud or discuss it with a peer, that meant it worked. The peer support activities were incredibly valuable because talking through problems helped me process them. Activities that were purely text-based or felt disconnected from actual understanding felt like busywork to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With limited resources and so many required tasks, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. Required activities had this weight of necessity that pushed me through even when my anxiety spiked. Optional ones, even when interesting, often got pushed aside when deadlines loomed. I wish I'd done more of them because they probably would have helped my exam scores.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I couldn't always afford textbooks, so I'd find free audio resources online or use text-to-speech on articles. I also recorded myself explaining concepts and played them back—sounds weird, but it really helped me learn. A lot of my studying happened with earbuds in, away from the computer.
Q10: So many situations! I'd be walking to the store listening to a downloaded lecture, or I'd be explaining concepts out loud to myself in my room. I had study sessions with peers over the phone that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. Sometimes I'd listen to the same recording five times while doing housework. The LMS would just show one download, but that represented hours of actual learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it repeatedly over the next week. With my limited internet situation, it made more sense to grab materials when I could and then study offline. I'd access a video, convert it to audio somehow, and then that single access represented days of actual engagement. The system couldn't see any of that continued work.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially around midterms when my anxiety was through the roof. I'd feel paralyzed sometimes, staring at the screen but unable to actually start anything. Financial stress from having low resources made it hard to focus—worrying about basics while trying to learn advanced concepts is exhausting. The peer support really helped pull me through those moments when I wanted to give up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Confusion in some courses made me avoid those materials entirely for a while. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't actually heard anything because my mind was elsewhere. My task completion suffered because sometimes I just couldn't push through the fog.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be racing with anxiety about exams or life stuff. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I was just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd re-watch things three times before actually processing them. The first two times were basically background noise while I stressed.
Q15: I postponed written assignments way more than I should have, which is probably why my assignment completion was only around 59%. Writing is harder for me than listening and discussing, so I'd avoid it until the last minute. My anxiety would build up around these tasks, making them feel even more impossible. I knew they were important, but knowing and doing are different things when you're anxious.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my life. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before things were due. My login patterns probably looked like a heartbeat monitor—spikes around deadlines, calmer in between. Deadlines gave me the external pressure I needed to push through anxiety, but they also meant I wasn't always learning deeply, just completing tasks. It wasn't ideal, but it kept me moving.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me, honestly. When I got positive comments, I'd feel motivated to engage more with that course. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me avoid that area for a while—not proud of it, but my anxiety would flare up. The peer feedback through our support system was often more helpful than formal feedback because it felt less judgmental and more collaborative.
Q18: The structure really mattered. Courses with clear audio components and discussion opportunities worked well for me. When things were well-organized with consistent patterns, my anxiety was lower and I could engage more fully. Courses that were chaotic or text-heavy made me feel lost and overwhelmed. I thrived when I knew what to expect and could plan my listening schedule around it.
Q19: Having recorded lectures available was a lifesaver—that made engagement so much easier. Discussion boards where I could interact with peers also helped tremendously. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on expensive textbooks I couldn't afford, or when everything was text-based with no audio options. Also, unclear instructions spiked my anxiety and made me avoid tasks entirely.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. Sometimes you're logging in constantly, watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand—but when it comes to actually producing work, you freeze. For me, the anxiety around assignments was way higher than the anxiety around consuming content. You can be genuinely trying to learn but still struggle to translate that into submitted work. It's not laziness; it's often fear.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people just learn efficiently outside the system—they download materials once and master them offline. Or they have strong prior knowledge and don't need as much engagement with course materials. I wish that were me, but honestly, I needed the high engagement just to keep up. My exam scores show that even with all my effort, something wasn't translating.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent, which gives a general picture of effort. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone was actually learning or just stressed-scrolling. They miss offline studying, audio learning while commuting, peer discussions over the phone. They can't capture anxiety freezing you in front of the screen or the breakthrough moment when something finally clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively listening, asking questions in my head, discussing with peers, trying to connect new ideas to what I already know. It's emotional too—feeling invested in my own learning journey despite the obstacles. True engagement is when I'm present mentally, not just logged in physically.
Q24: So much. The LMS can't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing dishes. It can't show the phone calls with classmates working through problems together. It can't capture my anxiety levels or how hard I fought to stay engaged despite financial stress and fear. It can't show that I showed up to 96% of classes, really trying, even when I was scared of failing. My effort was real, even if my exam score doesn't reflect it.
